[methods] Material and methods Animals This study utilized adult male C57BL/6J wild-type mice, heterozygous VGluT-cre (VGluT2-IRES-Cre: Slc17a6 tm2(cre)Lowl/J, The Jackson Laboratory, JAX stock 016963) and heterozygous VGat-ires-Cre transgenic mice (VGat-ires-Cre: Slc32a1 tm2(cre)Lowl/J, The Jackson Laboratory, JAX stock 016962). heterozygous Hcrt-ires-Cre mice were kindly donated by Dr. Yiyan Dong (Zhejiang University). Mice used in all experiments were housed in custom-made polycarbonate cages under controlled conditions: temperature (22 ± 1 °C), humidity (30–50%), and a stable 12-h light/dark cycle starting at 7 AM. Food and water were available ad libitum. At the start of the experiments, all mice were 8 weeks old and weighed between 23 and 30 grams. Experimenters conducting the behavioral tests were blinded to group assignments. Unless otherwise specified, behavioral experiments were conducted during the dark phase. At the end of the experiments, mice were anesthetized deeply with 5% sevoflurane and sacrificed by cervical dislocation. All procedures complied with standard ethical guidelines, as per the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Fudan University. Behavioral experiments Prior to experimentation, all mice underwent a 3-day habituation period, involving 2-min daily handling sessions. For all experiments, male mice were randomized and placed into groups based on weight. Behavioral experiments, excluding EEG/EMG recordings requiring 24-h data sampling, were conducted 1–4 h post-lights-off (20:00–24:00) under dim red light illumination. To eliminate olfactory cues, all apparatus were sanitized with 75% ethanol before and after each test. Behavioral tests were conducted in ascending order of stress induction, with a minimum inter-test interval of 24 h: open field test, elevated plus maze, sucrose preference test, tail suspension test, and forced swim test. A minimum 7-day recovery period was implemented before initiating subsequent behavioral test series. We used the same cohort of mice for the behavioral tests described above, and detailed temporal parameters for each behavioral test are provided in the results section and corresponding figure legends. To mitigate potential confounding effects of EEG/EMG electrode implantation on behavioral outcomes, separate cohorts of mice were utilized for EEG/EMG recordings. Assay for locomotor activity (open field test) The open field test was performed following established protocols [ 11 ], with minor modifications. Individual mice were placed in a white, cubic arena (40 × 40 × 40 cm) constructed of opaque plastic. Ambient illumination at the center of the arena was maintained between 30–60 lux. Spontaneous locomotor activity was recorded using an overhead digital camera. Locomotor parameters, including total distance traveled and average velocity, were quantified over a 30-min period using automated video tracking software (SMART3.0, Panlab, Barcelona, Spain). Assay for elevated plus maze test The elevated plus maze [ 24 ] (EPM) apparatus comprised two opposing open arms (35 × 6 cm) and two closed arms (35 × 6 cm, with 15 cm high walls) extending from a central platform (6 × 6 cm). The maze was elevated 70 cm above the ground level. Each subject was placed on the central platform facing an open arm and allowed to freely explore the maze for a 5-min period. Behavioral parameters quantified included the time spent in the open arms. The apparatus was sanitized with 75% ethanol between trials to eliminate olfactory cues. Assay for Tail-suspension test (TST) For the tail suspension test [ 39 ] (TST), mice were initially acclimated to the testing room. Subsequently, each mouse was suspended by its tail, secured with adhesive tape near the distal end, at a height of 50 cm from the ground. This elevation prevented any physical contact with surfaces or climbing behavior during the assessment. Behavioral responses were video-recorded for a 5-min duration. Immobility duration during the final 4 min of the test was quantified by a separate, blinded observer. Immobility was operationally defined as the absence of escape-oriented behaviors, including struggling or swinging motions. Assay for forced swimming test (FST) For the forced swim test [ 40 ] (FST), individual mice were placed in a transparent cylindrical apparatus (diameter: 25 cm, height: 40 cm) containing 15 cm of water maintained at 23–25 °C. The water depth precluded subjects from touching the bottom with their limbs or tail. Swimming behavior was video-recorded for a 6-min period. Immobility duration during the final 4 min was quantified manually by a blinded observer. Immobility was operationally defined as the period during which the subject remained floating passively or made only minor movements required to maintain its head above water. Assay for sucrose preference test (SPT) For the sucrose preference test [ 41 ] (SPT), mice were housed individually and provided access to two bottles: one containing water and the other a 1% sucrose solution for 48 h. The positions of the bottles were interchanged after 24 h. Following a 10-h water deprivation period, mice were presented with one bottle of 2% sucrose solution and one bottle of water for a 4-h test period. Bottle positions were interchanged midway through the test period. Sucrose preference was quantified as the ratio of sucrose consumption to total fluid intake (sucrose plus water). Pharmacological treatment For all pharmacological interventions, mice initially underwent behavioral testing following a vehicle injection. Subsequently, after a 7-day washout period, the same cohort was administered the drug and subjected to behavioral testing. Behavioral assessments were repeated on the same cohort one week post-drug discontinuation. In the case of the 3-week Li-H 2 O regimen, additional behavioral testing was conducted 14 days after treatment cessation. Baseline behavioral assessments were performed in the absence of pharmacological intervention during this period. Detailed timelines for behavioral testing are provided in the respective results sections. Mice received intraperitoneal injections of either sodium valproate (ApexBio, USA; 200 mg/kg in saline), lithium chloride (Sigma-Aldrich; 100 mg/kg in saline), or saline vehicle. For acute lithium or valproate administration (multiple injections), mice initially underwent behavioral tests (locomotion and tail suspension) following a vehicle injection. After a 7-day interval, the same cohort was administered lithium or valproate. For the Li-H 2 O paradigm [ 11 ], mice were administered lithium chloride via drinking water (300 mg/L) over a 3-week period. Baseline behavioral assessments were conducted with mice on regular water, followed by a 3-week Li-H 2 O regimen for the same cohort. Valproate and lithium treatments adhered to established protocols [ 42 , 43 ], involving two injections at 4-h intervals on the day preceding and the day of behavioral testing. Behavioral assessments were performed 1 h post-final injection on the test day. Statistical analyses and quality control Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism (GraphPad Prism 9; GraphPad Software, Inc.). Sample sizes was determined based on our previous experience with assay variance and experimental feasibility. Normality was tested by the Shapiro-Wilk test. Equal variances were assessed by F-test. For datasets that were normally distributed, parametric tests of the variance and group differences (paired, unpaired t-tests, one-way or two-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s, Bonferroni’s or Tukey’s post hoc tests.) were used. Otherwise, the non-parametric Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, and Mann Whitney Rank Sum Test were used. For the analysis of correlations between the LS ablation size and mania-like behaviors, we used the Pearson correlation for normally distributed data. Data are represented as the mean ± s.e.m. Statistical significance was set at four levels (* P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, *** P < 0.001, **** P < 0.0001). If histological results do not confirm significant expression of mCherry in the target brain region, or if the expression significantly extends beyond the target area, or if there is a malfunction in the EEG/EMG recording technique during the experiment, the mouse will be excluded from the analysis. All the experiments were performed and analyzed blind to treatment conditions. Detailed information about the materials and methods is provided in the supplementary material.

[results] Chemical inhibition or chronic ablation of LS GABAergic neurons resulted in social hyperactivity and irritability Increased social presence is observed during manic episodes in humans. In the three-chamber social test (S Fig. 6A ), we recorded the activity trajectories of the mice over a 10-min period and presented representative total tracks of the control group, LSr GABA -hM4Di mice, and LSr GABA -Casp3 mice in the chambers (S Fig. 6B ). The results showed that both LSr GABA -hM4Di and LSr GABA -Casp3 mice had a significantly higher sociability index (SI) compared to the control group (S Fig. 6C, D ). Irritable responses in manic patients are usually an over-amplification of ordinary stimuli, even to small external changes or threats. Therefore, we next investigated irritable behavior induced by tactile and physical stimuli by subjecting each mouse to two consecutive manual tactile and two physical stimuli using plastic sticks, with 1-min intervals of rest after 35 min of habituation in a home cage (S Fig. 6E ). The scoring criteria for behavior have been described previously40 and consist of 5 parameters: freezing, standing, hyperventilation, aggression, and avoidance. Each parameter is scored from 0 to 2, and the total score reflects the degree of defensive response. Both response scores were significantly increased in LSr GABA -hM4Di mice and LSr GABA -Casp3 mice compared to control mice (S Fig. 6F, G ), indicating that these mice exhibited irritable behavior.

[discussion] Discussion Extensive research has been dedicated to elucidating the neurobiological mechanisms of mania [ 55 ]. Nevertheless, the precise neural circuitry underlying mania-like behaviors remains elusive. Our study identified a specific brain region that mediates the development of mania-like behavior. Specifically, ablation or acute inhibition of LS GABAergic neurons can produce phenotypes similar to mania, including hyperactivity, elevated mood, reduced sleep, increased socialization, and irritability. Notably, these mania-like effects are attenuated by frontline clinical mood stabilizers, suggesting a potential correlation between the observed behaviors in our mouse model and human manic episodes in bipolar disorder. However, it remains unclear whether the manic phenotype is collectively produced by the disinhibition of multiple downstream target brain regions following ablation to or acute inhibition of LS GABAergic neurons. We further traced the downstream brain regions of LS GABAergic neurons and found that inhibition of the LS-LH projection induced a manic effect (hyperactive, anxiolytic and antidepressant), while inhibition of the LS-HDB projection induced partial manic effects, including increased locomotion and anxiolysis. In conclusion, our findings demonstrate that inhibition or loss of LS GABAergic neurons, particularly those projecting to the LH, plays a critical role in the manifestation of mania-like behaviors (S Fig. 13 ). The LS has been identified as a nexus of emotion and motivation, harboring abundant GABAergic neurons that regulate locomotion, mood fluctuations, and sleep-wake behaviors [ 56 ]. Behavioral hyperactivity following LS ablation primarily results from the disruption of the LS and its associated circuits. Theta oscillations in the hippocampus and LS, coordinating their primary subcortical outputs, modulate motor coding. Both chemical and optogenetic inhibition of dorsal hippocampal-LS projections lead to increased running speed. Consistent with our observations, stimulation of theta rhythms in LS projections to the LH decreases running speed, highlighting the critical role of the LH in locomotion control [ 57 ]. Furthermore, several studies have demonstrated that stimulation or inhibition of neuronal activity in the LS can induce or alleviate anxiety, respectively [ 58 , 59 ]. Our previous studies have demonstrated that acute stress activates GABAergic neurons in the LS, inducing anxiety-like behaviors [ 24 ]. Gray and McNaughton proposed that the LS plays not only an important role in emotion regulation but also a crucial role in the modulation of behavioral inhibition, impulse control, and social interaction behaviors [ 60 ]. Therefore, damage to the LS may lead to the loss of behavioral inhibition (disinhibition), manifested as impulsive and aggressive behaviors, which is consistent with the behaviors we observed in the model, such as hyperactivity, sociability, and irritability. Moreover, a reduced sleep requirement is a fundamental hallmark of mania, and many factors associated with disease relapse may trigger manic episodes through significantly decreased sleep [ 61 – 63 ]. Our study revealed that chronic ablation of GABAergic neurons in the LS induced multiple forms of sleep disturbances in mice, including increased wakefulness after sleep onset, reduced sleep requirement, unstable sleep-wake cycles, and fragmented sleep (Fig. 3 ). These findings align with clinical observations in patients with bipolar disorder [ 64 ]. Interestingly, our study demonstrated that delta power increased following LS ablation, which is consistent with clinical observations of enhanced delta/alpha EEG activity in patients with bipolar disorder [ 65 ]. During manic episodes, patients often experience sleep deprivation, reduced sleep efficiency, or fragmented sleep. The observed increase in delta power may represent a compensatory rebound mechanism, in which the brain attempts to restore disrupted neural homeostasis by enhancing slow-wave sleep. In addition, since delta oscillations are generated by synchronized discharges between the cortex and thalamus [ 66 ], damage to the LS may impair the modulation of these thalamo-cortical circuits, leading to abnormal amplification or dysregulation of delta rhythms. Given the potential bidirectional relationship between sleep abnormalities and mood symptoms [ 67 ], the fragmented sleep observed after LS dysfunction may not merely be a concomitant of manic-like behaviors. Instead, it could actively contribute to emotional dysregulation through interactions between arousal systems and limbic circuits, thereby exacerbating mood instability. Our findings also indicate that mood stabilizers, such as lithium and valproic acid, partially or significantly alleviate mania-like symptoms. Administration of mood stabilizers (lithium or valproate) to rats reversed these mania-like behaviors [ 68 ], aligning with our findings and further validating this model. Lithium is an effective treatment for acute mania and relapse prevention. It preferentially targets excitatory neurons and accumulates inside cells via voltage-gated channels and neurotransmitter-coupled channels. Within the cell, lithium ions replace sodium ions at a 1:1 ratio, leading to a decrease in intracellular sodium concentration, thereby reducing neuronal excitability [ 69 ]. When voltage-gated sodium channels open, valproate inhibits the activity of these channels, exerting selective effects on the activity-dependent firing of rapidly firing neurons [ 70 ]. Consequently, valproate primarily suppresses active neurons, whereas lithium accumulates throughout the neuron, affecting depolarization of the soma and firing in the axon. Our experiments show that acute single-dose administration can partially alleviate manic-like behavior, suggesting that these drugs may exert short-term effects by rapidly modulating neural circuit activity, such as the GABA/glutamate balance [ 35 – 38 ]. Long-term use, however, may lead to more sustained effects through mechanisms like gene expression modulation and synaptic remodeling [ 71 ]. Therefore, exploring novel pharmacological interventions that target activated LS neurons for the treatment of manic episodes in bipolar disorder represents a promising avenue for future research. An important issue regarding the role of the LS is the functional differences among its various subnuclei. Distinct subregions of the rodent LS have been delineated based on cellular structure, neuroanatomical connections, and the expression of various neurotransmitters [ 72 , 73 ]. However, a comprehensive understanding of the functional specialization of LS subregions remains elusive, both in rodents and humans. The phenomenon of septal rage induced by LS ablation typically necessitates damage to the rostral LS [ 29 ]. Corroborating this spatial specificity, we found that inhibition of caudal LS GABAergic neurons failed to elicit mania-like behaviors (Fig. 1 ). In the present study, inhibition of rostral LS GABAergic neurons induced excessive arousal and disrupted sleep homeostasis. Conversely, our previous research demonstrated that inhibition of dorsal-intermediate LS GABAergic neurons attenuates arousal via the midbrain dopamine circuit [ 74 ]. These findings suggest that there are functional differences among LS subnuclei in modulating sleep-wake phenotypes. The LSc expresses somatostatin, substance P receptor, and arginine vasopressin 1a receptor, while the LSr expresses neurotensin, enkephalin, Type 2 corticotrophin-release factor receptor (CRFR2), and dopamine 3 receptor [ 75 ]. Recent optogenetic studies have shown that activation of CRFR2-positive neurons in the LS produces anxiogenic effects in the light–dark box, open field, and NO tests, and this action depends on monosynaptic projections from LS CRFR2 neurons to GABAergic neurons in the anterior hypothalamic area [ 76 ]. In contrast, activation of somatostatin-positive interneurons in the LSc exerts anxiolytic effects in the EPM [ 77 ]. Thus, different neuronal subpopulations within the LS exhibit functional heterogeneity in anxiety regulation. Ongoing research into the emotional functions of the LS, particularly with emphasis on the functional roles of distinct LS subregions and different neuron types, will facilitate the development of a cohesive model of LS function and provide more precise targets for potential applications in the treatment of psychiatric disorders. The LS provides extensive neural innervation to brain regions that regulate different behaviors. Employing viral tracing and chemogenetic neural control, we identified the downstream targets of LS GABAergic neurons that mediate mania-like behavior, notably the LH and the HDB. The LH comprises orexin/glutamate neurons that facilitate arousal, motivation, and energy expenditure [ 78 – 80 ], alongside GABAergic neurons whose activation elicits arousal and locomotion [ 81 , 82 ]. We observed that activation of LS GABAergic terminal projections suppressed LH GABAergic neurons, suggesting that LH GABAergic neurons may receive direct inhibitory projections from the LS and play a key role in the manifestation of mania-like behavior. Corroborating our findings, a previous study demonstrated that electrical stimulation of the LH at 180 Hz successfully induced mania-like behavior in rats, characterized by hyperactivity and reduced rest time [ 27 ]. Interestingly, we observed that activation of LS GABAergic neurons led to the activation of LH orexinergic neurons, which may reflect a disinhibition mechanism triggered by the suppression of LH GABAergic neurons. These results suggest that LS GABAergic neurons exert differential modulation on LH neuronal populations, highlighting the heterogeneity of LH cell clusters. Future studies involving more detailed neural manipulation of LH cell clusters may provide valuable insights into the neural network mechanisms underlying mania. Several limitations of the present study warrant discussion. First, this study was conducted exclusively on male mice. While male patients exhibit a higher incidence of hypomania or mania compared to females [ 83 ], it is crucial to address gender differences in the manifestation and treatment efficacy of bipolar disorder [ 84 , 85 ]. Consequently, future studies should incorporate female mice to address this limitation. Second, a notable limitation is the absence of lithium concentration monitoring during pharmacological treatment. In clinical practice, monitoring lithium serum levels is crucial for the safe administration of this drug [ 86 ]. Nonetheless, the dosage and duration of lithium administration in this study were based on previous research to minimize toxicity risk [ 11 ], and no abnormal behaviors were observed in the mice during treatment. Third, although we have preliminarily demonstrated the role of GABAergic neurons in the rostral LS in mediating mania-like behaviors, we have not yet defined the specific contributions of finer neuronal subpopulations within this region (such as neurotensin-positive neurons) to the mania-like phenotype. This will be a key direction for future investigations. Finally, similar to other studies on mania-like behaviors [ 50 , 68 , 87 , 88 ], our research does not fully capture the entire spectrum of mania-related symptoms observed in all patients, nor does it reflect the transition between manic and depressive states [ 10 ]. In addition, anxiety- or depression-related tests that rely on locomotor activity, such as the open field test, may be confounded by the elevated activity levels per se. However, these limitations are common to most existing models used in mania research and are not unique to the present study. Despite these constraints, our model demonstrates strong cross-species comparability and translational potential in terms of behavioral phenotypes, pharmacological responses, and underlying neural circuit mechanisms. In summary, our study demonstrated that aberrations in neuronal plasticity constitute the neuropathological basis for the generation of mania-like behavior, specifically manifesting as defects in the LS and its associated hypothalamic circuitry. This enhanced comprehension of these neural circuits is expected to facilitate the development of novel therapeutic strategies that target the underlying pathophysiological mechanisms of bipolar disorder.

retrieved_papers.title: Increased levels of SNAP-25 and synaptophysin in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex in bipolar I disorder.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2006]
[Abstract] In order to identify whether the mechanisms associated with neurotransmitter release are involved in the pathologies of bipolar disorder and schizophrenia, levels of presynaptic [synaptosomal-associated protein-25 (SNAP-25), syntaxin, synaptophysin, vesicle-associated membrane protein, dynamin I] and structural (neuronal cell adhesion molecule and alpha-synuclein) neuronal markers were measured in Brodmann's area 9 obtained postmortem from eight subjects with bipolar I disorder (BPDI), 20 with schizophrenia and 20 controls.
Determinations of protein levels were carried out using Western blot techniques with specific antibodies. Levels of mRNA were measured using real-time polymerase chain reaction.
In BPDI, levels of SNAP-25 (p < 0.01) and synaptophysin (p < 0.05) increased. There were no changes in schizophrenia or any other changes in BPDI. Levels of mRNA for SNAP-25 were decreased in BPDI (p < 0.05).
Changes in SNAP-25 and synaptophysin in BPDI suggest that changes in specific neuronal functions could be linked to the pathology of the disorder.

[Introduction] 1 Introduction Bipolar disorder (BD) is one of the devasting mood disorders influenced by manic-depressive neuropsychiatric condition affecting ∼2 % of the global population ( Kessler et al., 2005 ). Despite the fact that neurotransmitter systems manages both visceral and behavioural symptoms of mood, BD may be a condition of disrupted synapses and circuits, particularly in the region of prefrontal cortex of the brain. Several pathophysiological observations anticipated BD as a cause of cellular injury and/ or due to dysregulated cellular mechanisms. Lithium (lightest of all metals) explicit intense manifestation to control BD progression via specific mechanisms underlying cellular circuitry remains a hot topic of study ( Tondo and Baldessarini, 2009 , Muller-Oerlinghausen and Lewitzka, 2010 ). As a monovalent cation, lithium weighs half the density of water and is considered to be a standard treatment regimen for BD over the past 60 years. It works via inhibiting inositol polyphosphate1-phosphatase (IPP), and inositol monophosphatase-1 (IMPA1) resulting accumulation of high quantity inositol 1-phosphates in the neuronal cavity. This inhibition eventually reduces myo-inositol assembly and lowers the level of cellular inositol triphosphate thus act as a key regulatory factor to the secondary messenger synthesized by the membrane bound phosphor-inositides ( Williams and Harwood, 2000 , Beaulieu et al., 2009 ). In this way lithium influence the glycogen synthase kinase-3 (Gsk3), and/or Akt (protein kinase B) signaling mechanisms ( Williams and Harwood, 2000 , Baldessarini and Tondo, 2000 ) to affect several neurotropic factors ( Gould et al., 2004 , Wada, 2009 ) such as the cytoskeletal framework, metabolic mechanisms, and gene expression control ( Chen et al., 2010 , Beaulieu et al., 2009 ). Several studies on animal models (Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Huntington’s disease (HD), and Parkinson’s disease (PD) with spinal cord injury evident that lithium administration can facilitate protection against neurodegeneration. This stimulatory effect is known to endorsed via cellular and metabolic pathways that explicit effective transformation of neurotransmitters in the synaptic regions ( Bosetti et al., 2002 , Weerasinghe et al., 2004 , Yin et al., 2006 , Chepenik et al., 2010 , Pavuluri et al., 2010 , Narita et al., 2011 ). Several studies have demonstrated that lithium decreases the levels of SNAP-25 (synaptosomal associated protein-25) and MAP2 in normal rats' prefrontal cortex cells (PCC) ( Lakshmanan et al., 2012 ). This is consistent with the documents rise in SNAP-25 in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) of individuals having bipolar I illness ( Scarr et al., 2006 ). Existing reports suggest that the formation of numerous synaptic vesicles (SV) may elicit the root cause to the development of BD. As the synthesis of SV soly depend on the interaction between syanpsin and cytoskeletal components, determining the integrity of neural cytoskeleton underlying PCC remains an interesting area of analysis. In this context it has been established that, the cytoskeletal protein MAP2 can be a signature choice to investigate the therapautic efficacy of lithium in BD rat model system.

It works via inhibiting inositol polyphosphate1-phosphatase (IPP), and inositol monophosphatase-1 (IMPA1) resulting accumulation of high quantity inositol 1-phosphates in the neuronal cavity. This inhibition eventually reduces myo-inositol assembly and lowers the level of cellular inositol triphosphate thus act as a key regulatory factor to the secondary messenger synthesized by the membrane bound phosphor-inositides ( Williams and Harwood, 2000 , Beaulieu et al., 2009 ). In this way lithium influence the glycogen synthase kinase-3 (Gsk3), and/or Akt (protein kinase B) signaling mechanisms ( Williams and Harwood, 2000 , Baldessarini and Tondo, 2000 ) to affect several neurotropic factors ( Gould et al., 2004 , Wada, 2009 ) such as the cytoskeletal framework, metabolic mechanisms, and gene expression control ( Chen et al., 2010 , Beaulieu et al., 2009 ). Several studies on animal models (Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Huntington’s disease (HD), and Parkinson’s disease (PD) with spinal cord injury evident that lithium administration can facilitate protection against neurodegeneration. This stimulatory effect is known to endorsed via cellular and metabolic pathways that explicit effective transformation of neurotransmitters in the synaptic regions ( Bosetti et al., 2002 , Weerasinghe et al., 2004 , Yin et al., 2006 , Chepenik et al., 2010 , Pavuluri et al., 2010 , Narita et al., 2011 ). Several studies have demonstrated that lithium decreases the levels of SNAP-25 (synaptosomal associated protein-25) and MAP2 in normal rats' prefrontal cortex cells (PCC) ( Lakshmanan et al., 2012 ). This is consistent with the documents rise in SNAP-25 in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) of individuals having bipolar I illness ( Scarr et al., 2006 ). Existing reports suggest that the formation of numerous synaptic vesicles (SV) may elicit the root cause to the development of BD. As the synthesis of SV soly depend on the interaction between syanpsin and cytoskeletal components, determining the integrity of neural cytoskeleton underlying PCC remains an interesting area of analysis. In this context it has been established that, the cytoskeletal protein MAP2 can be a signature choice to investigate the therapautic efficacy of lithium in BD rat model system.

[methods] 2 Methodology of literature search An extensive review was performed to the major publications relevant to the clinical aspects of lithium dependent BD treatment effects, its mechanistic role of neurons via neuroimaging studies linked to BD patients during progressive treatment. A comprehensive literature search was conducted via Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, and Science Direct using the keywords such as “lithium” and “bipolar disorder” and “therapeutic mechanism of action” and “clinical use” and “microtubular” and “brain imaging” and “brain structure” and “cytoskeleton” and “mood episodes” and “neuropsychiatric diseases”. Essential findings were synthesized from the literature that are futher evaluated to acquire additional publications. In order to achieve technological advancements in BD treatment, this study was passed from any timescale plans. The selection of language was restricted to English. The search obtained 485 articles, of which 330 were review publications. A manual search was conducted to all essential publications including bibliographies in order to identify any omitted articles. Articles irrelevant to the aim of the current study, language other than English, duplicate files, short-communications, magazine papers, letters, were excluded. Publications met within the inclusion criteria were screened for further analysis. 2.1 Cell stress in neurons, atropy and neuronal loss Changes associated with the loss of brain structure and volume is considered to be the most devasting charasteristics features of BD pathogenesis. Several atopsy reports and brain imaging studies obtained via magnetic resonance spectroscopy [MRS] of BD subjects claimed decreased brain volume directly to the site of mood regulation. Consistent evidences suggest that this reduction in the brain density is due to neuronal/glial stress, atrophy, and death associated with neurons ( Manji & Duman, 2001 ). A study led by Ongur and team identified substantial reduction of glial cell density (41.2 %) in the prefrontal cortex of BD subjects with a family history of mood disorders. Similar results were consisently discovered in other studies with decreased grey matter in distinct areas of the brain where emotional and cognitive processess are controlled ( Brambilla et al., 2005 ). Such abnormalities were generally seen in the subgenual prefrontal and anterior cingulated cortex region, hippocampus, and basal ganglia of the CNS (central nervous system). Deep scanning (MRS) images revealed abnormal synthesis of N-acetyl-aspartate (NAA), myoinisotol, and choline in BD specimens. Methodologically, these are potential markers that act via neurotrophic pathways and respond during lithium administration in treatment regulations. Unlike other neurodegenerative diseases, BD is considered as a non-classic form of brain disease due to the absence of gliosis, a specific marker found in neurodegenration. However in BD, patches of possible glial loss anticipated with NAA elevation and choline reduction were common ( Yildiz-Yesiloglu & Ankerst, 2006 ). NAA synthesis in mitochondria is associated with altered cell’s energy regulation and has been considered as a crucial parameter in neuronal stress, apoptosis, and altered gene expression that ultimately result in neurotrophic effects. Neuronal survival is facilitated by several neurotrophins including brain derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), neurotrophin-3 (NT-3), NT-4, NT-65, NT-6, and nerve growth factor (NGF). In BD, continuous diminution of these factors strongly affect neuronal viability. Declined levels of BDNF were reported in depressive and manic episodes of human subjects that strides BDNF as a potential biomarker to identify neuronal vulnerability. Both, in vivo and in vitro studies explicit equal contribution of CREB & BDNF in mediating the therapeutic and neurotrophic efficacy of lithium. However, dysregulation of any targets of BDNF-CREB could eventually leads to atrophy to any selective subpopulation of the neurons. Therefore, deep research is needed to elucidate the kinetics behind neurotrophins mediated cell fate network during BD progression. 2.2 Neurotrophic effect of lithium in BD Lithium typically stabilizes mood swings in BD with a cyclic process of administration followed by different episodes of depression. However, its effect may no longer exhibit when applicable in the reverse manner ( Puglisi-Allegra et al., 2021 ). In a contray, lithium can less react in conditions like depressive susceptibility, drug addiction, psychotic syndromes, and fast cycling. Due to this, lithium has been advocated to treat major depressive disorder (MDD) since the late 20th century as an alternate pharmacotherapy for antidepressant-resistant individuals ( Abou-Saleh et al., 2017 , Undurraga et al., 2019 ). Currently, there is no conclusive data comparing the effectiveness of lithium monotherapy to other depressive problems such as short-term depressive episodes ( Sato & Hattori, 2018 ). Nonetheless, numerous studies have demonstrated the beneficial usage of lithium after it has been administrated as an additional agent against depression therapy. Therefore lithium was proposed as an alternative agent to treat MDD patients who failed to respond for any antidepressants ( Iwahashi et al., 2021 , Sanchez et al., 2000 ). This effort has been proposed to constantly reduce suicidal risks in both unipolar and bipolar victims ( Malhi et al., 2013 ) possibly acting on GABA ( Malhi et al., 2013 ) by regulating autophagy through modulation of aggression and impulsivity ( Beurel & Jope, 2014 ). In prophylaxis cases of manic and bipolar depression, lithium shows promising effects. This indeed pose majority of the treatment guidelines recommended lithium as the first-line therapy ( Geddes et al., 2010 ). On the other hand, some randomized studies based on controlled trials (RCT) to patients diagnosed with bipolar disorder I (BD-I) with depressive, manic symptoms and/or a mixture of both were reported to have significant increase in the recurrence rate when compared to treatments involved with placebo ( Weisler et al., 2011 ). Conversely, open labelled randomized trial studies to BD-1 patients with no longer chronic episodes underwent long-term treatment to prove high relief on lithium-monotherapy when compared to that of valproate monotherapy ( Geddes et al., 2010 ). These observations indicate the prophylactic efficacy of lithium to treat manic conditions than that of depressive episodes ( Curran & Ravindran, 2014 ). Several studies have suggested that lithium can control bipolar psychosis via BD which is often associated with manic episodes and psychotic features ( Fornaro et al., 2016 ). However, limited studies were conducted for systematic assessments on lithium to treat both mania with psychosis. Comparative studies of various neutoleptics against lithium based treatment to I and II-level episodes of mania and psychosis revealed chlorpromazine as an effective medication to treat psychotic features ( Prien et al., 1972 ). Similarly, the drug sripiprazole was also promising than lithium and placebo in treating psychotic features ( Keck et al., 2009 ), where lithium was found effective as that of quetiapine and superior than placebo in treating psychosis with manic episodes ( Bowden et al., 2005 ). Lithium monotherapy is considered to have high significance than placebo to treat manis in psychotic subtype ( Swann et al., 2002 ). It also produce early improvements in psychotic symptoms, and exhibit similar efficacy to both psychosis and non-psychosis mania ( De Sousa et al., 2012 ). It has been long recognized that lithium can modulate immune responses and explicit unique features to empasis affective disorders by altering proinflammatory cytokine synthesis and through GSK-3β inhibition ( Rybakowski et al., 2018 ). In addition to acute clinical effect of lithium, it is assumed that long term treatments could enhance clinical effects such as inflammatory suppression, neuroprotection of brain cells, and prevention of depressive episodes. Studies using structural neuroimaging and MRI analysis revealed close association between lithium treatment that had increased grey matter volume of the affected victims than untreated patients ( Phillips et al., 2008 ). In a similar study, examinations among 17 lithium treated and 12 untreated BD patients showed significant increase in the quantity of white and grey matter ( Bearden et al., 2008 ). Other clinical reported have showed enhanced grey matter density in the cortical regions of the brain that had a greater volume of hippocampal in lithium-treated patients. Specified evaluations on potential targets such as NAA and myoinositol followed by four weeks of lithium administration resulted increased NAA levels in distinct regions of the brain and reduced levels of choline and myoinositol ( Silverstone et al., 2003 ). This suggest that lithium has a strong neurotrophic effect on BD patients. One of the most replicating findings identified significant reduction of anterior cingulate volumes of the brain of BD patients compared to their healthy volunteers ( Sassi et al., 2004 ). 2.3 Lithium toxicity and adherence Despite the global importance of lithium as an effective therapeutic substance, its direct usage is restricted due to the growing margin of toxicity ( Gitlin, 2016 ). Self-administration of lithium often results increased dose index that emphasize nearly 20–27 % hospitilzed poisoning and stimulated death rates of 10–25 % ( Camus et al., 2003 ). Dose dependence of lithium may therefore vary among individuals and can be determined by indexing the pharmacokinetics and risk of intoxication. Therefore the recommended usage of lithium is calculated based on the difference between its excretion rate in individuals and the percentage of serum concentration levels. Usually, 10–80 mmol with a plasma concentration of 0.4–1.2 mmol/L is recommended for effective treatment practices ( Couffignal et al., 2017 ). Increase in the serum concentration (<1.2 mmol) is considered potentially lethal and life threatning. Care should be taken during lithium administration and periodic monitoring of blood serum levels will ensure safety from adverse effects. Another persistent problem associated with lithium administration is non-adherence of patients to the prescription. It is expected that discontinuation of lithium intake leads to adverse effects ( Table 1 ). Henceforth, new strategies must be developed to improve regular followups to ensure unnecessary termination of lithium treatment. Table 1 Chronic effects of lithium (Drug doses). Table 1 S.No Chronic effects common (< 10 %) Lesser common chronic effect (>10 %) Reference Weight gain Polyuria Thirst Diarrhea Tremor Reduced emotional and /or perceptual intensity a Impairment of Cognitive a Acne Nausea a Psoriasis Hypothyroidism a Kidney impairment b Cardiac arrythmia Hyperparathyroidism ( Mendiola et al., 2021 ) a Dose related b Total lithium represents the availability of lithium over time and its serum-lithium concentration to the drug-dose level 2.4 Molecular mechanism of lithium Lithium is known to modulate multiple cellular pathways but the exact mechanism underlying has not been fully elucidated. Several studies have suggested that lithium can specifically target neurological events to enrich neuroprotection and modulate mood stabilizing activities ( Malhi et al., 2013 ). However, various hypotheses explicit that lithium action can determine cell-fate and drive glycogen metabolism to confer BD treatment ( Price and Heninger, 1994 , Goodwin and Jamison, 1990 , Berridge et al., 1989 , Avissar et al., 1988 ). In this, the inositol depletion hypotheses model was highly accepted among all mechanisms where lithium inhibits inositol uptake and prevents IMPase (inotitol monophosphatase) and IPPase (Inositol polyphosphate-1-phosphatase) resulting in IP3 depletion of cells that acts as an endogenous source of inositol ( Berridge et al., 1989 , Avissar et al., 1988 , Maeda, 1970 ). Cells that are incapable to receive external signals fail to produce InsP3 (inositol 1,4,5 triphosphate) and therefore block the InsP3-dependent response ( Fig. 1 ). This stimulates ER stress to release huge quantity of Ca 2+ . Lithium inhibits apoptosis and autophagy linked to ER stress, deposit dysfunctional mitochondria and prevent proteins from misfolding. This results accumulation of ROS leaked from mitochondria that downregulate the nuclear factor erythroid 2 (NFE2) favoring mitophagy and mitochondriogenesis. Abnormal unfolded protein response (UPR) upregulates TRAF/JNK, and XBP1favoring apoptosis during autophagy process, whilst, lithium triggers downstream of these pathways by inhibiting PKC and GSK3β via series of pathways. Lithium facilitate GSK3β inhibition and impair neurotrophic transcription, neuroprotective, and inhibit the expression of antioxidant genes (NRF2, Bcl-2 [B-cell lymphoma 2], VEFG, and BDNF), to produce proinflammatory cytokines via STAT, INFℽ, and NF-kβ activation to reconstruct the altered cytoskeleton. Fig. 1 Summarize the molecular events that takes place by lithium in specific cell target. Fig. 1 In the other way, the GSK-3 mechanism that regulates synaptic plasticity and cell-apoptotic events favor the progression of cell-death. Therefore, the attenuated characteristics of this pathway might regulate cellular resilencing and neuroplasticity ( Carter, 2007 ). Any alteration to the GSK-3 mechanism might affect the antimanic and antidepressant activity via gene regulation ( Martinowich et al., 2009 , Serretti et al., 2008 , Lee and Kim, 2011 ). Hence, lithium stimulation can influence various neuroprotective pathways by increasing the GSK3β phosphorylation and migating the effects of excitotoxicology ( Table 2 ) ( De Sousa et al. 2015 ). Studies have identified that lithium can respond positively via GSK3β gene expression pathway and phosphorylation to control the BD symptoms. In addition, lithium can directly inhibit GSK3 activity and indirectly affect Akt activation pathway ( Iwahashi et al., 2014 ). This action will suppress the behavioural effect of DA transmission via β-arr2-mediated Akt/GSK-3 signaling mechanism. These evidences suggest that the therapeutic effect of lithium anticipate sensitization to BD subtype in the similar way to its phenotype ( De Sousa, Streck, et al., 2015 ). Changes occur in the intracellular calcium levels might play significant role in modulating neurotransmitter transmission in the synaptic region. This is associated with mitochondrial function to explicit neuroplasticity, calcium balance, and apoptosis activity ( Neher & Sakaba, 2008 ). However, a dysregulated oxidative metabolism could rely on mitochondrial damage and favor destruction of genetic material that eventually leads to neuronal apoptosis ( Gigante et al., 2011 , Konradi et al., 2004 ). Similarly, the dysfunctional mitochondrial samples of BD patients show expression of hippocampal genes associated with destructive mitochondrial proteins ( Konradi et al., 2004 ). In addition, enhanced telomere shortening occurring in BD patients have great influence due to stress-related oxidative damage ( Simon et al., 2006 ). In fact, several cellular mechanisms associated with lithium favor antioxidant expression ( Macedo et al., 2013 ), are known to suppress the assembly of stress proteins ( Nciri et al., 2013 ), delay in the synthesis of proinflammatory substances ( Wang et al., 2013 , Watanabe et al., 2014 , Myint and Kim, 2014 , Kim et al., 2007 ), and affect gene expression associated with oxidative cytoprotection ( Rizak et al., 2014 ). Several observations declare that administration of lithium in BD patients could results reduced lipid peroxidation levels ( De Sousa et al., 2014 ), enhanced mitochondrial function and reverse the stimulation of oxidative stress pathways ( De Sousa, Streck, et al., 2015 ). It is expected that lithium can also associate with secondary messenger factors to enhance its therapeutic potential via pathways including protein kinases C, phosphoinositide cycle, and intracellular calcium production ( Malhi & Outhred, 2016 ). Lithium ameliorate multiple fuctions such as ameliorating mitochondrial dysregulation via inositol depletion and inhibiting IMPA1 ( Sarkar et al., 2005 ), and provoke neuronal plasticity by blocking protein kinase ‘C’ ( Tsui et al., 2012 ). Lithium can downregulate TRPC3 (transient receptor potential channel 3) to maintain Ca 2+ homeostasis ( Zaeri et al., 2015 ) and stimulate c-AMP pathway ( Heinrich et al., 2013 ). These observations infer lithium as an effective therapeutic agent to act upon several cellular pathways to stabilize neuroprotection in BD patients.
